title: Part 7: The Silencing — In David Fried’s Courtroom, Advocacy Itself Has Become a Risk
content:
By The Ethics Reporter

The language of the law is, by design, stripped of emotion. Transcripts are meant to record facts, not feelings—a neutral ledger of what was said, by whom, and when. But the transcript from a January 28, 2026, inquest in a Rockland County courtroom, presided over by Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried, reads like something else entirely. It reads like a document of intimidation.

The hearing was, on its face, routine. A non-jury inquest in a civil matter—one of hundreds that move through Rockland County Supreme Court every year. The plaintiff’s attorney, a Muslim woman, had submitted a letter the night before explaining that she was dealing with a personal emergency and would be appearing remotely via Microsoft Teams. In most courtrooms, this kind of advance notice is a professional courtesy that judges extend without comment. In Fried’s courtroom, it became a provocation.

Before a single fact in the underlying case had been addressed, Fried turned his attention to the letter. Her correspondence, he announced, “doesn’t make anything accurate at all.” Then, for page after page of transcript, he berated her—not for anything she had done in the matter before him, but for the timing and tone of her communication with chambers. At some point during the lecture, she attempted to preserve her objections for the record. Fried’s response was immediate. He attacked her for trying to “create a record.”

In any other courtroom, this phrase would be unremarkable. Creating a record—ensuring that arguments, objections, and procedural facts are documented for appellate review—is not a privilege extended to attorneys. It is an obligation. It is among the most fundamental duties a lawyer owes her client. When Fried wielded the phrase as an insult, he was not being imprecise. He was being precise about something else entirely: his expectation that the attorneys before him do not advocate too zealously on behalf of the people who hired them.

The January hearing was unusual enough to prompt a bar complaint against Fried, filed by The Ethics Reporter. What followed was more unusual still. Rather than recusing himself from related matters or quietly addressing the complaint, Fried responded with a court order.

On March 30, 2026, Fried issued his decision in Adler v. Pollak (Index No. 035769/2025). The opinion was ostensibly a legal judgment. In practice, it was something closer to a settlement of personal accounts. Paragraphs were devoted not to the merits of the case but to characterizing the attorney—the same attorney whose treatment had triggered the ethics complaint—as unprofessional, obstructive, and deserving of financial punishment. Attorneys who have reviewed the order describe it as unlike any judicial writing they have seen: pointed, personal, and unmistakably retaliatory.

Fried also understood that he had an audience. Under New York’s Part 130 rules (22 NYCRR 130-1.1), formally sanctioning an attorney for frivolous conduct requires a judge to issue an Order to Show Cause—a procedural step that notifies the attorney, guarantees her a hearing, and creates a paper trail subject to appellate scrutiny. Had Fried gone that route, the attorney could have challenged not only the sanction, but the conduct underlying it. His own January hearing would have been placed under examination. So he found a different route. By classifying the financial penalty as “costs” under CPLR § 6514(c), Fried bypassed the Order to Show Cause entirely. The attorney was penalized without a hearing. The mechanism designed to protect attorneys from exactly this kind of judicial overreach was neatly avoided.

The legal community in Rockland County is not large. Attorneys who practice in local courts know each other, know the judges, and know what is tolerated and what is not. Word travels. The pattern of what has happened in Fried’s courtroom—the January confrontation, the retaliatory order, the financial penalty issued without due process—has not gone unnoticed. What has gone unsaid, in the way things often go unsaid when the person to be offended is a sitting judge, is that the pattern changes the calculus for every attorney who appears before him.

Zealous advocacy, which is what the bar requires of lawyers, is also what Fried punished. The attorney who appeared on January 28th did nothing her ethics rules did not demand. She showed up for her client under difficult circumstances. She attempted to protect the record. She submitted a letter explaining why. These are not provocations; they are the basic mechanics of legal representation. That Fried treated them as provocations is precisely the point.

An adversarial legal system depends on lawyers who are willing to push back—on opposing counsel, on unfavorable facts, and yes, on judges. Appellate courts exist because trial courts make mistakes, and the record reviewed on appeal is only as complete as the lawyers had the freedom to make it. When a judge signals that creating a record is unwelcome, he is not just managing his courtroom. He is shaping what justice looks like after he renders it.

None of this happened without context. Fried’s political career in Rockland County stretches back decades—through the town courts of Ramapo and Spring Valley, through a 2013 county executive campaign shadowed by reports of campaign favors from a convicted slumlord, through his elevation to the New York State Court of Claims by Governor Kathy Hochul in 2023, and through documented connections to associates of Jeffrey Epstein. His associations and his temperament have been catalogued at length in this series. What Part 7 adds to that portrait is simpler, and in some ways more consequential: a picture of what it looks like when a judge’s personal grievances begin to govern whether justice is actually accessible.

The question Fried’s conduct raises is not abstract. It is asked, quietly and without comfort, by every attorney in Rockland County who has a client, a case, and an appearance on his calendar.

In Part 8, we trace David Fried’s authoritarian tendencies back to their origins in Ramapo and Spring Valley—and examine what his political rise reveals about the systems that allowed it to happen.
excerpt: Part 7 of The Ethics Reporter's 10-part investigation into Rockland County Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried.

title: Part 6: Subverting the CPLR: David Fried’s Assault on New York Legal Procedure
content:
To understand the depth of the transgression committed in the chambers of Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried, you must first understand the physical weight of the New York Civil Practice Law and Rules. Known colloquially among attorneys as the CPLR, it is typically published as a massive, red-bound volume, dense with tissue-thin pages of statutory text and annotations. It is not an exciting book. It is a masterpiece of bureaucratic engineering, a sprawling, granular operating system designed to govern every conceivable mechanism of civil litigation in the state of New York. It dictates how lawsuits are filed, how subpoenas are served, how time is measured, and how disputes are ultimately resolved. For a functioning judiciary, the CPLR is the guardrail that prevents the adversarial system from descending into anarchy. For Judge David Fried, facing the exposure of his own misconduct, the CPLR became something entirely different: a weapon of convenience, picked up in a panic and swung with malicious intent.

There is a specific architecture to judicial discipline in New York. If a judge believes an attorney has engaged in frivolous conduct—if they are wasting the court's time, harassing opposing counsel, or making a mockery of the proceedings—the judge possesses the authority to punish them. The mechanism for this punishment is found in Part 130 of the Rules of the Chief Administrator. Part 130 is a severe tool, allowing a judge to impose financial sanctions on a lawyer. But because the power to strip a citizen of their money is profound, Part 130 is wrapped in the ironclad protections of due process. A judge cannot simply wake up angry and issue a fine. They must issue an Order to Show Cause. They must provide formal, written notice to the attorney. And, most crucially, they must hold a hearing.

A hearing requires a record. It requires the attorney to be allowed to speak, to present evidence, and to defend herself in open court. It is the fundamental bedrock of American jurisprudence: the right to be heard before being condemned.

In the spring of 2026, David Fried had a profound problem. After his unhinged berating of a Muslim female attorney during a January 28 Microsoft Teams inquest—a hearing in which he attacked her simply for trying to preserve a record of his hostility following her personal emergency—he found himself the subject of a formal bar complaint filed by The Ethics Reporter. The structural silence he had relied upon throughout his political ascent in Rockland County was collapsing. The transcripts were public. His temperament was exposed.

Fried wanted to retaliate. He wanted to punish the attorney for the audacity of survival, for refusing to be bullied into submission. He wanted to hit her where it hurt: her finances and her professional reputation. He decided on a fine of $3,500, to be paid to opposing counsel. But to issue that fine legally, he had to use Part 130. To use Part 130, he had to issue an Order to Show Cause and hold a hearing.

Fried knew that a hearing was the one thing he could not survive. If he convened a formal proceeding to sanction the attorney, she would have the absolute right to introduce the transcript of the January 28 Teams call into evidence. She would place his own abusive words, his own volatile demeanor, formally onto the record in a setting where he could not simply mute her microphone. A Part 130 hearing would not be a trial of her conduct; it would become an undeniable, documented indictment of his own.

So, the judge made a calculated, desperate choice. He decided to bypass the law entirely.

On March 30, 2026, Fried issued his retaliatory Decision and Order. In it, he engaged in a breathtaking act of judicial contortion. He explicitly stated that he was declining to issue a formal sanction under Part 130, a tacit admission that he dared not trigger the due process requirements the rule demanded. Having abandoned the actual legal mechanism designed for attorney discipline, Fried reached blindly into the vast, dense pages of the CPLR to find something—anything—he could use to execute his punishment without a hearing.

He landed on CPLR Section 6514(c).

To fully appreciate the absurdity of this choice, one must understand what CPLR § 6514 actually does. Article 65 of the CPLR deals exclusively with real estate. Specifically, it governs the "Notice of Pendency," historically known as a lis pendens. If two parties are fighting over the ownership of a house, one party can file a Notice of Pendency to freeze the property, preventing it from being sold while the lawsuit is ongoing. Section 6514 dictates how that notice can be canceled. Subsection (c) of that rule states that if someone files a Notice of Pendency maliciously or in bad faith, tying up a property without justification, the judge can cancel the notice and award costs to the victimized property owner.

The case of Adler v. Pollak, the matter before Judge Fried on January 28, had absolutely nothing to do with a maliciously filed Notice of Pendency. It was not a dispute over a frozen real estate transaction. The attorney had not tied up a property deed. She had submitted an emergency letter the night before a virtual inquest because of a personal crisis.

Yet, in his March 30 order, Judge Fried cited CPLR § 6514(c) as the sole legal authority to hold the attorney and her entire law office "jointly and severally liable" for $3,500 in costs.

It was an act of judicial malpractice so brazen it bordered on the surreal. It was the equivalent of a traffic cop citing a driver for maritime piracy because he couldn't find his radar gun. Fried took a statute designed to protect homeowners from fraudulent real estate liens and weaponized it to fine a lawyer for requesting a scheduling adjournment.

The cruelty of the March 30 order was not merely in its financial penalty or its defamatory language. The true malice lay in its procedural architecture. By explicitly rejecting Part 130 and shoehorning his retaliation into the irrelevant confines of CPLR § 6514(c), Fried achieved his primary objective: he bypassed the Order to Show Cause. He bypassed the notice. He bypassed the hearing. He stripped the attorney of her right to defend herself, ensuring that the damning transcript of January 28 would not see the light of day in his courtroom. By labeling the $3,500 punishment as "costs" under a real estate statute rather than a formal "sanction," he actively sought to sabotage her ability to appeal his conduct through the standard appellate pathways designed for disciplinary review.

When a judge misinterprets the law, it is an error, and the appellate courts exist to correct it. But when a judge knowingly dismembers the law, taking statutes wildly out of context to inflict personal vengeance and evade accountability, it is something far more dangerous. It is the transformation of the bench into a weapon of the state, wielded by a man who has confused the authority of his office with the divine right of kings.

The big red book of the CPLR is designed to ensure that the power of the state is applied predictably, fairly, and with due process. David Fried opened that book, tore out a page on real estate, and used it to bludgeon a woman who had dared to stand up to him. In doing so, he did not merely abuse an attorney; he indicted himself. He proved, in writing and under his own signature, that he possesses neither the temperament to judge nor the integrity to be trusted with the law.
excerpt: Part 6 of The Ethics Reporter's 10-part investigation into Rockland County Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried.

title: Part 5: Judicial Misconduct in Plain Sight: Why David Fried Must Be Investigated by the Judicial Conduct Commission
content:
In the quiet, administrative corridors of New York state’s judicial oversight apparatus, grievances generally go to die in the dark. The New York State Commission on Judicial Conduct, headquartered in a nondescript office in Manhattan with branches upstate, receives thousands of complaints every year. They arrive in manila envelopes, in hastily typed emails, in frantic phone calls from litigants who believe they have been wronged and attorneys who know they have been abused. The vast majority of these complaints are dismissed outright. They are categorized as routine appellate issues, or the sour grapes of the losing party, or simply matters lacking the documentary weight required to challenge the state's most insulated class of public officials. For decades, the Commission has operated as a quiet pressure valve, maintaining the illusion of accountability while largely protecting the sanctity and the silence of the bench.

To understand the structural silence that surrounds the judiciary in New York, one must understand the unique vulnerability of the practicing attorney. A lawyer’s livelihood is entirely dependent on the grace of the court. To file a formal complaint against a sitting judge is to invite professional suicide. Judges talk. They attend the same bar association dinners, they golf at the same country clubs, they operate within a tightly knit fraternity that instinctively recoils at the sight of a whistleblower. An attorney who reports a judge for misconduct knows that they will likely have to appear before that same judge, or their colleagues, again. The retaliation is rarely overt. It is a motion denied here, a scheduling conflict ignored there, a subtle but pervasive chilling of the attorney’s practice. Because of this, the Commission on Judicial Conduct is starved of its most crucial resource: the testimony of the lawyers who actually witness the abuse.

This historical toothlessness has bred a specific kind of judicial hubris, nowhere more evident than in the political ecosystem of Rockland County, and nowhere more embodied than in the career of Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried. Fried is a product of the Ramapo and Spring Valley political machine, an environment where political survival often requires a certain flexibility with the rules and a profound understanding of patronage. His trajectory is a testament to the fail-safes built into the New York political apparatus for those who play the game. In 2009, his campaign for local office operated out of a building owned by a notorious slumlord, a scandal that would have derailed a less connected politician. In 2013, he made a failed bid for Rockland County Executive. He later found a comfortable landing spot working as an Assistant to the Sheriff for Public Safety and Counterterrorism. Along the way, he cultivated connections with individuals deeply embedded in the county's power structures, including associates of the late Jeffrey Epstein—a revelation recently brought to light by The Ethics Reporter. Through it all, Fried remained untouched, eventually ascending to the Town of Ramapo and Village of Spring Valley justice courts.

Then came June 2023. In a move that highlighted the profound failures of the state's judicial vetting process, Governor Kathy Hochul appointed Fried to the New York State Court of Claims, effectively elevating him to Acting Supreme Court Justice. The appointment was a triumph of machine politics over rigorous scrutiny. It sent a clear message to the legal community of Rockland County: David Fried was untouchable. He had ascended to the highest trial court in the state, insulated by the Governor’s seal of approval and the historical inertia of the Commission on Judicial Conduct.

But hubris has a way of leaving a paper trail.

On the morning of January 28, 2026, the structural silence of the Rockland County courthouse fractured. The venue was a routine civil inquest in the matter of Adler v. Pollak, conducted via Microsoft Teams. The plaintiff’s attorney, a Muslim woman, had experienced a severe personal emergency the night before. Following standard protocol, she had submitted a letter to the court detailing the emergency and requesting an adjournment. In a functioning judicial system, such a request, especially from an attorney dealing with a sudden crisis, is handled with basic professional courtesy. But David Fried’s courtroom had long ceased to function on the principles of basic professional courtesy.

When the attorney appeared on the Teams screen, visibly distressed and attempting to manage her crisis while fulfilling her ethical obligation to her client, Judge Fried did not offer a continuance. Instead, he unleashed a barrage of hostility. He attacked her for submitting the letter. He berated her professional conduct. The transcript of the hearing reads less like a judicial proceeding and more like a targeted psychological assault. When the attorney, drawing on her training and attempting to protect her client’s rights for an inevitable appeal, calmly tried to preserve her objections on the record, Fried’s anger escalated. He attacked her explicitly for trying to "create a record." It was a naked display of judicial intimidation—an attempt to bully an attorney into abandoning her duty to her client out of fear of the judge’s wrath.

For most attorneys, the encounter would have ended there. They would have muted their microphones, swallowed their indignation, and advised their clients that the judge was simply having a bad day. They would have calculated the cost of filing a complaint with the Commission on Judicial Conduct, weighed it against their future in Rockland County, and chosen silence. It is the calculation that thousands of attorneys make every year. It is the calculation that allows judges like David Fried to operate with impunity.

But the attorney in Adler v. Pollak did not remain silent. And crucially, she was not alone. The Ethics Reporter obtained the transcripts and the correspondence, documenting the abuse not as hearsay, but as empirical fact. In February 2026, The Ethics Reporter published the details of the hearing, exposing the machinery of intimidation that Fried had utilized. More importantly, The Ethics Reporter bypassed the terrified local bar and filed a formal bar complaint against Judge Fried directly with the Commission on Judicial Conduct.

The filing of the complaint fundamentally altered the dynamic. The Commission was no longer dealing with the whispered complaints of an anonymous lawyer. They were presented with public, documentary evidence of a judge explicitly attacking an attorney for attempting to preserve an appellate record—a fundamental violation of a litigant's right to due process.

Faced with exposure, Judge Fried had a choice. He could have recused himself. He could have recognized that his temperament had compromised his impartiality. Instead, he chose retaliation. On March 30, 2026, David Fried issued a Decision and Order that will likely serve as the defining document of his judicial downfall.

The March 30 order was not a legal document; it was a weaponized tantrum disguised in the formatting of a judicial decree. In it, Fried explicitly defamed the plaintiff's attorney, twisting the events of January 28 into a narrative that portrayed him as the victim of her supposed insolence. But the true malice of the order lay in its mechanism of punishment. Fried wanted to sanction the attorney, to inflict financial pain for her audacity. However, formal sanctions under Part 130 of the New York Codes, Rules and Regulations require due process: an Order to Show Cause, formal notice, and a hearing where the attorney can defend herself.

Fried knew he could not survive a public hearing. He knew that if he issued an Order to Show Cause, the attorney would have the opportunity to present the transcript of the January 28 hearing, exposing his abuse on the record. So, he bypassed the rules entirely. He declined to issue a Part 130 sanction. Instead, he contorted a completely unrelated statute—Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) Section 6514(c), a provision designed for the cancellation of a Notice of Pendency in real estate disputes—to hold the attorney and her law office jointly and severally liable for $3,500 in opposing counsel's "costs."

By issuing the March 30 order, Judge Fried committed the cardinal sin of corrupt authority: he put the corruption in writing. The order was explicitly retaliatory, legally nonsensical, and procedurally void. It was an attempt to use the financial power of the bench to punish an attorney who had dared to report him, and to do so in a way that circumvented her right to appeal the conduct through normal disciplinary channels.

This is why the Commission on Judicial Conduct finds itself at a historical crossroads. For decades, it has justified its inaction by pointing to the ambiguity of the complaints it receives. It has hidden behind the veil of "judicial discretion," arguing that a judge's temperament on the bench is a matter of personal style rather than ethical misconduct. But there is no ambiguity in the transcript of January 28. There is no judicial discretion in the retaliatory order of March 30.

David Fried has handed the Commission the exact type of documentary evidence it has always claimed it requires to take action. He has demonstrated, on the public record, a fundamental inability to separate his personal vindictiveness from his judicial duties. He has shown that he views the law not as a framework for justice, but as a blunt instrument to be wielded against those who challenge his authority. If the Commission fails to act on this—if it looks at the transcripts, the retaliatory order, the explicit weaponization of the CPLR, and decides that David Fried is still fit to wear the robe—then it is not merely toothless. It is complicit.

The machinery of silence in New York's judiciary relies on the assumption that nobody is watching, and that even if they are, nobody cares. David Fried operated under that assumption for his entire political and judicial career. He survived the slumlord scandals, the Epstein connections, the machine politics of Ramapo. He was elevated by the Governor. He believed he was immune. But the ink on the March 30 order is dry, and the transcripts cannot be unwritten. The shadows where grievances usually go to die have been flooded with light, leaving the judge, and the Commission that oversees him, with nowhere left to hide.
excerpt: Part 5 of The Ethics Reporter's 10-part investigation into Rockland County Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried.

title: Part 4: The Loophole Judge: How David Fried Weaponizes "Costs" to Evade Due Process and Malfeasance Claims
content:
The machinery of the New York State unified court system is vast, grinding, and largely invisible to the public it serves. It operates on a foundation of procedure, an arcane language of statutes and rules designed, in theory, to remove human emotion from the administration of justice. But procedure is only as neutral as the person wielding it. In the hands of a diligent jurist, the civil practice rules are a roadmap to fairness. In the hands of a judge acting out of spite, ego, or the raw panic of impending exposure, those same rules become a labyrinth, a weapon, and a trap. To understand the anatomy of judicial retaliation, one need only look at the gap between what a judge is allowed to do and what a judge can get away with. On March 30, 2026, Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried found that gap, and he drove a $3,500 punishment right through it.

The collision that triggered this maneuver occurred two months earlier, on the morning of January 28, 2026. The venue was the civil inquest for Adler v. Pollak (Index 035769/2025). The courtroom had been replaced by the flattened, sterile grid of a Microsoft Teams screen. The plaintiff’s attorney, a Muslim woman, logged into the hearing carrying the weight of a sudden personal emergency. She had done what is generally expected in a profession that frequently collides with the unpredictability of human life: she had submitted a letter to the court the night before, explaining her situation and seeking the court’s grace. In a functional courtroom, governed by a judge with a secure sense of authority, the letter would have been acknowledged, the calendar adjusted, or at the very least, the proceedings managed with a modicum of professional courtesy.

But David Fried was not running a functional courtroom. When the attorney appeared on the screen, she was not met with the measured demeanor of a State Supreme Court justice. She was met with the unleashed hostility of a machine politician who felt his absolute authority was being questioned. Fried attacked her. He berated her for the letter, his voice radiating the kind of performative outrage that often masks deep insecurities. When the attorney, maintaining her composure under fire, attempted to do the one thing every law student is taught to do when facing a hostile bench—preserve her objections for the appellate record—Fried’s anger calcified into something far more dangerous. He attacked her explicitly for trying to "create a record." For an arrogant judge, there is no greater offense than a lawyer who remembers that the judge, too, is being watched, and that the transcript is forever. The attempt to create a record is a reminder of accountability, and David Fried’s entire career had been an exercise in avoiding exactly that.

The January 28 tirade might have vanished into the ether of the court’s digital archives, just another bad day in the basement of the justice system, had the silence not been broken. In February 2026, The Ethics Reporter published a detailed account of the hearing, dragging Fried’s courtroom conduct out of the shadows and into the harsh light of public scrutiny. The exposure was followed by action: The Ethics Reporter filed a formal bar complaint against Justice Fried. Suddenly, the dynamics of power in Rockland County were inverted. The judge who was accustomed to ruling without consequence found himself the subject of an investigation. For a man who had climbed the political ladder from the slumlord-funded campaign offices of Ramapo to the insulated heights of the state bench, this was not just an inconvenience. It was an existential threat.

There is a specific, toxic alchemy that occurs when an unaccountable figure is suddenly forced to account for themselves. The reaction is rarely contrition; it is almost always retaliation. Fried could not directly attack The Ethics Reporter, a journalistic entity protected by the First Amendment. But he had a captive target: the attorney who had dared to preserve the record. He had the power of the bench, and he had the Civil Practice Law and Rules. On March 30, 2026, two months after the Teams hearing and squarely in the aftermath of the bar complaint, Fried issued his Decision and Order in Adler v. Pollak. What should have been a standard adjudication of civil matters was instead transmuted into a vehicle for professional assassination.

The March 30 Order was a remarkable document, not for its jurisprudence, but for its malice. In its pages, Fried actively defamed the attorney, attempting to codify his January 28 temper tantrum into the permanent, unassailable ink of a judicial finding. But defamation alone was not enough; he needed to inflict financial and professional pain. To do this, he had to navigate a very specific legal hurdle: the rules governing judicial sanctions.

In New York, if a judge wishes to punish an attorney for frivolous or improper conduct, the standard mechanism is found in Part 130 of the Rules of the Chief Administrator. A Part 130 sanction is a formal, serious reprimand. But because the power to sanction is so severe, the law requires guardrails. To issue a Part 130 sanction, a judge cannot simply declare a lawyer guilty by fiat. The judge must issue an Order to Show Cause (OSC). An OSC is a fundamental requirement of due process. It demands that the judge provide formal notice to the attorney, detailing exactly what they are accused of, and, crucially, it guarantees the attorney a hearing to defend themselves.

David Fried knew exactly what an Order to Show Cause meant. An OSC meant a hearing. A hearing meant testimony. And testimony meant that the transcript and the video of the January 28 Teams call—the very evidence of his own abusive, unhinged behavior—would become the focal point of the proceeding. He could not survive a Part 130 hearing because the evidence would exonerate the attorney and indict the judge. To punish her, he had to find a way to circumvent the constitutional requirement of due process. He had to find a loophole.

The loophole he found was buried in the administrative minutiae of the civil code: CPLR § 6514(c). In his March 30 Order, Fried explicitly and strategically declined to issue a formal sanction under Part 130. Instead, he pivoted. Relying on CPLR § 6514(c), he declared that the attorney and her law office were jointly and severally liable for $3,500 in "costs."

The distinction between a "sanction" and "costs" might seem like semantic hair-splitting to a layperson, but in the tactical warfare of the courtroom, it is the difference between vulnerability and invincibility. By framing the financial penalty as "costs" rather than a "sanction," Fried successfully bypassed the requirement for an Order to Show Cause. There would be no notice. There would be no hearing. There would be no opportunity for the attorney to play the tape of January 28, no chance for her to defend her reputation on the record. With a single stroke of the pen, Fried acted as accuser, prosecutor, judge, and jury.

The sum—$3,500—was calibrated with malicious precision. It was not a number large enough to bankrupt a law firm or generate the kind of immediate, high-profile appellate outrage that a six-figure penalty might provoke. But it was large enough to sting, and by making the attorney and her office jointly and severally liable, Fried ensured that the professional humiliation was distributed among her colleagues. It was a message sent not just to her, but to every attorney practicing in his court: this is what happens when you attempt to create a record. This is what happens when someone files a complaint against me.

The maneuver was a masterclass in the dark arts of procedural abuse. By exploiting the gap between Part 130 and CPLR § 6514(c), Fried effectively insulated his retaliatory strike from the standard avenues of appellate review. When an attorney appeals a Part 130 sanction, the appellate court looks at the record of the hearing. But because Fried bypassed the hearing, the appellate record was sterile, populated only by his own defaming Order and the silence he had forced upon his victim. He had weaponized the rules of civil procedure to construct a reality where his abuse was legally codified and the attorney’s defense was procedurally erased.

This is the ultimate danger of a political machine elevating an unqualified operative to the bench. A judge like David Fried does not simply break the rules; he learns how to use the rules to break people. The March 30 Order was not an act of jurisprudence; it was an act of vengeance, dressed in the respectable camouflage of legal citation. It was a demonstration of how a system built to dispense justice can be effortlessly hijacked to dispense cruelty, so long as the person at the controls understands exactly which levers to pull.

The attorney, who had logged in on a Tuesday morning carrying a personal emergency and asking for basic human grace, found herself ambushed by a judge who had never learned the difference between authority and bullying. The $3,500 penalty was not restitution for the court’s time; it was the price she was forced to pay for Governor Kathy Hochul’s failure to vet her appointments. It was the toll exacted by a political culture in Rockland County that rewards loyalty over integrity. David Fried had spent his entire career learning how to operate in the shadows of the law, and on the bench of the State Supreme Court, he finally found the perfect place to hide.

title: Part 3: From Slumlords to Epstein Associates: The Troubling Political History of Rockland County Judge David Fried
content:
In the geometry of local politics, the campaign headquarters is a temporary citadel. It is usually a leased storefront with papered-over windows, smelling faintly of stale coffee, industrial carpet adhesive, and the desperate, electric hum of ambition. It is meant to be the physical embodiment of a candidate’s promise to the community—a beacon of accessibility and forward momentum. But in 2013, when David Fried was mounting his campaign for Rockland County Executive, his political architecture was built on a foundation that was, quite literally, decaying. The space he occupied was owned by a figure notorious in the local community as a slumlord, a landlord whose properties were synonymous with peeling paint, chronic violations, and the silent suffering of tenants who had nowhere else to go. This was not merely a matter of poor real estate judgment; it was a profound illustration of the cognitive dissonance required to climb the ladder of New York municipal politics.

To understand David Fried’s eventual ascent to the bench of the New York State Supreme Court, one must first understand the geography of his origins. Rockland County is a complex ecosystem, a dense, suburban wedge caught between the gravitational pull of New York City and the sprawling quiet of the Hudson Valley. Within it lie the Town of Ramapo and the Village of Spring Valley, jurisdictions that have long operated as insular fiefdoms. Here, politics is less about grand ideological battles than it is about the raw, unglamorous mechanics of patronage, demographic alliances, and the strategic distribution of favors. In this environment, a candidate does not rise purely on the merits of their jurisprudence or the purity of their civic vision. They rise because they understand how the machine works, and more importantly, they understand whom the machine protects.

The 2013 slumlord scandal was a flare illuminating the dark machinery of Fried’s political operation. When you are running to be the chief executive of a county, every association is a signal. Renting space from a landlord known for exploiting the county's most vulnerable residents sent a clear, if unintentional, message about where the campaign’s true allegiances lay. The optics were disastrous, but in the cloistered world of Ramapo politics, optics are often treated as an inconvenience rather than a disqualification. The controversy, which had roots stretching back to his earlier political maneuvers in 2009, revealed a candidate who was deeply embedded in a network of power brokers who operated with impunity. It was an early warning sign of a foundational arrogance—a belief that the rules of accountability, the very expectations of ethical hygiene, simply did not apply to him.

This network of alliances extended into shadows that were only fully brought to light years later. In February 2026, The Ethics Reporter broke the story of Fried’s connections to associates of Jeffrey Epstein. The revelation was jarring, yet to observers of the Rockland County machine, it possessed a grim, inevitable logic. The Epstein network was, at its core, an enterprise that preyed upon vulnerability while insulating itself through proximity to power. How does a local political figure brush against such an apparatus? It happens not in grand conspiracies, but in the mundane transactions of campaign finance, the quiet dinners, the introductions made in the VIP tents of charity galas. It happens when a political ecosystem becomes so insular, so detached from the scrutiny of a robust press, that the only currency that matters is influence, regardless of its origin. The fact that these connections remained submerged for so long speaks volumes about the structural silence that protects the ambitious in local politics.

Following the bruising realities of electoral campaigns, Fried needed to launder his public image. He needed a title that conferred unimpeachable authority, something that washed away the grit of slumlord landlords and backroom deals. He found it in the Rockland County Sheriff’s Office. The title he acquired was almost comically grandiose: Assistant to the Sheriff for Public Safety and Counterterrorism. In a county primarily known for its shopping malls and commuter rail lines, "Counterterrorism" sounds less like a functional job description and more like a line of dialogue from a network television procedural. Yet, it served a vital, transformative purpose. It allowed Fried to wrap himself in the solemn, unquestionable mantle of law enforcement. The badge, the proximity to the machinery of state security, the gravitas of "public safety"—all of it acted as an ablution. He was no longer just a political operative navigating the messy terrain of Ramapo; he was a guardian of the public order.

This transition was the necessary prelude to his ultimate destination: the judiciary. Fried’s judicial career began in the subterranean levels of the justice system—as a justice for the Town of Ramapo and the Village of Spring Valley. Local justice courts are the basements of American jurisprudence. They are often crowded, poorly lit rooms where the dockets are punishingly long and the stakes, while seemingly small, are life-altering for the people involved. Evictions, traffic violations, small claims, minor infractions—these are the venues where the state exercises its power over the working class, the poor, and the marginalized. In these rooms, a judge wields a kind of absolute, unquestioned authority that rarely exists in higher courts. There are no appellate lawyers hovering in the gallery; there is rarely even a court reporter to document the nuance of a ruling or the tone of a reprimand.

It is in these local courts that judicial temperament is either forged or corrupted. For a judge inclined toward arrogance, the local bench is a dangerous training ground. It teaches them that their word is absolute law, that their impatience is justified, and that the people standing before them are not citizens seeking justice, but annoyances clogging the schedule. Fried spent years marinating in this environment, a local magistrate operating within the protective bubble of the Ramapo machine. He learned the rhythms of unaccountable power. He learned that he could berate, dismiss, and control the narrative of his courtroom without consequence, because the people he was berating rarely had the means to fight back. The structural silence of the political machine extended naturally into the structural silence of his courtroom.

The system is designed to catch flaws before they are elevated, but the system is run by politicians. In June 2023, Governor Kathy Hochul needed to make judicial appointments. The process of elevating a judge to the New York State Supreme Court is meant to be an arduous, merit-based gauntlet, requiring rigorous vetting of a candidate’s history, temperament, and ethical standing. However, there is a well-worn loophole in the architecture of New York’s judiciary: the Court of Claims. Governors frequently use the Court of Claims as a side door. By appointing an ally to this specialized court, the administration can immediately designate them as an "Acting Supreme Court Justice," granting them the prestige and the docket of a Supreme Court judge without the necessity of running in a complex, multi-county election.

When Hochul appointed David Fried to the State Court of Claims in June 2023, the vetting process failed in spectacular, systematic fashion. The committees reviewing his background either missed or actively ignored the red flags that had defined his career. They looked past the 2009 and 2013 slumlord scandals. They either did not find or did not care about the whispers of Epstein-adjacent associates. They failed to scrutinize the imperious, volatile temperament he had cultivated on the bench in Ramapo and Spring Valley. The Commission on Judicial Conduct, an agency with a long, depressing history of toothlessness, had done nothing to interrupt his ascent. Hochul’s administration, eager to satisfy the political demands of the Rockland County machine, rubber-stamped his elevation. It was a triumph of patronage over prudence.

And so, David Fried was handed the robes of a Supreme Court Justice. He was relocated from the crowded, chaotic basement courts of Spring Valley to the hushed, wood-paneled authority of the state’s highest trial court. He was granted an extraordinary amount of power over the lives, liberties, and fortunes of the citizens of New York. But the robes did not change the man; they merely amplified him. The man who had learned to navigate the compromised ethics of Ramapo real estate, who had cleansed his resume with a hollow law enforcement title, and who had learned to rule as a petty tyrant in local justice courts, was now insulated by the awesome prestige of the state. He was untouchable, wrapped in the protective layers of Albany’s blessing.

The architecture of his career was a testament to how power protects power. Every step of his journey, from the campaign office to the counterterrorism desk to the bench, was facilitated by a political machine that valued loyalty over integrity. The structural silence had worked perfectly. No one had stopped him. No one had held him accountable. He had learned the most dangerous lesson a public official can learn: that the rules are malleable, that consequences are for other people, and that authority is not a responsibility, but a weapon.

This was the man who took the bench on the morning of January 28, 2026, to preside over the inquest in the civil matter of Adler v. Pollak. He sat high above the courtroom, a product of a system that had taught him he was invincible. When a Muslim woman—an attorney who had experienced a personal emergency and submitted a letter to the court the night before—appeared via Microsoft Teams to represent her client, she was not stepping into a neutral forum of justice. She was stepping into the culmination of David Fried’s political and judicial history. She was stepping into a room where the judge believed he could do whatever he wanted, because, for his entire career, he always had.

title: Part 2: Sociopathy on the Bench? Analyzing Rockland County Judge David Fried’s Inquest Transcripts
content:
The digital courtroom on the morning of January 28, 2026, was devoid of the physical grandeur that usually masks the brutality of the justice system. There were no polished mahogany benches, no vaulted ceilings, no bailiffs standing at attention. There was only the sterile interface of Microsoft Teams, a grid of small rectangles through which the fate of the litigants in the civil matter of Adler v. Pollak (Index 035769/2025) would be decided. In one of those rectangles appeared the plaintiff’s attorney, a Muslim woman and seasoned litigator. In the center, dominating the screen and the proceeding, was Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried.

To read the transcript of that morning’s inquest is to study the anatomy of judicial bullying. It is a masterclass in how power is abused not through sweeping legal pronouncements, but through a thousand petty, vindictive cuts. The transcript strips away the veneer of impartial adjudication, revealing a judge who viewed the courtroom not as a forum for resolving disputes, but as a stage for his own aggrieved ego.

The context of the hearing is crucial. The attorney had experienced a sudden, severe personal emergency the night before. Caught between her duty to her client and the reality of her crisis, she did what the rules of professional conduct dictate: she immediately submitted a letter to the court, explaining her situation in detail, requesting an accommodation, and subsequently appearing for the inquest virtually. It was a standard, necessary communication, the kind of procedural housekeeping that occurs dozens of times a day in courts across New York State. It should have elicited, if not sympathy, then at least professional courtesy. Instead, it became the spark that ignited David Fried’s fury.

From the moment the record opened, the atmosphere was toxic. Fried did not merely deny her request; he went on the offensive. The transcript reveals a judge obsessed not with the merits of the civil dispute before him, but with the perceived slight to his authority. He attacked the attorney for the letter itself. He castigated her for daring to have an emergency that inconvenienced his calendar, treating her correspondence not as a plea for necessary accommodation, but as an act of insubordination.

“You sent a letter,” Fried’s words bleed through the sterile black-and-white of the court reporter’s transcript. The tone, as described by those present, was dripping with contempt. He proceeded to dissect her communication, not to analyze its legal sufficiency, but to humiliate her on the record. He belittled her crisis, minimizing her emergency while maximizing his own outrage. It was a calculated degradation, designed to remind everyone on the Microsoft Teams call exactly who held the power.

The attorney, maintaining the disciplined composure forged through years of practice, attempted to navigate the onslaught. She tried to explain. She tried to pivot the focus back to the needs of her client. But for a judge intent on asserting dominance, composure is often interpreted as defiance. Her calm professionalism only seemed to enrage Fried further.

Judicial bullying rarely occurs in isolation; it is a symptom of a broader pathology. When a judge berates an attorney, he is also poisoning the well for the client. He is signaling to the opposing counsel that it is open season. He is creating an environment where the law is secondary to the judge’s emotional volatility. In Adler v. Pollak, the plaintiff was watching as her advocate, the person standing between her and financial ruin, was systematically degraded by the person entrusted to deliver justice.

As the inquest proceeded, the attorney realized the proceeding was irreparably tainted. Fried’s hostility was not merely personal; it was actively prejudicing her client’s case. Recognizing her absolute professional obligation, she began to do what any competent litigator must do in such a situation: she attempted to preserve the record. She raised objections to Fried’s characterizations. She tried to articulate the legal and factual basis for her positions, ensuring that an appellate court reading the transcript months or years later would understand exactly what transpired.

Preserving the record is the fundamental mechanism of appellate review. It is the only defense an attorney has against a rogue judge. If an objection is not raised, it is waived. If the record is silent, the appellate court assumes the judge acted properly. But to a judge like David Fried, an attorney attempting to preserve the record is not performing a professional duty; she is challenging his infallibility.

When the attorney began to state her objections, Fried erupted. The transcript captures a judge losing all semblance of judicial temperament. He cut her off. He talked over her. And then, he delivered the line that encapsulates the entire sordid affair: he attacked her for trying to “create a record.”

He said it with undisguised venom. By attacking her for “creating a record,” Fried was implicitly demanding that she abdicate her responsibility to her client. He was demanding that she submit to his abuse in silence, leaving no trace of his misconduct for a higher court to review. He was, in essence, demanding that she participate in the cover-up of his own judicial malfeasance.

This is the chilling reality of practicing law before a judge who knows he is unaccountable. The attorney was trapped in a Catch-22 designed by Fried himself. If she remained silent, she failed her client and waived her right to appeal. If she spoke up, she faced the immediate wrath of a judge willing to use his power to punish her. She chose to speak. She chose to endure the berating in order to fulfill her oath.

Reading the transcript, one is struck by the stark contrast between the two participants. On one side is an attorney, facing a personal crisis, attempting to navigate a hostile environment with professionalism and grace. On the other side is a judge, wielding the immense power of the State of New York, behaving with the petulance of a tyrant whose absolute authority has been momentarily questioned.

The January 28 inquest did not end when the Microsoft Teams connection was severed. The echoes of Fried’s tirade lingered in the digital ether, a permanent stain on the record of Adler v. Pollak. But the true damage was structural. Every time a judge behaves like this and faces no consequences, the system degrades. Attorneys learn to cower. Clients lose faith. The concept of a fair and impartial judiciary becomes a punchline.

The transcript of that morning is not just a record of a single, ugly hearing. It is a mirror held up to a system that elevates men like David Fried—men with histories of scandal, men with powerful political connections, men who view the bench not as a public trust, but as a private fiefdom. It is a document that asks a profound and troubling question: When the arbiter of justice is the architect of the abuse, where does one turn?

For the Muslim attorney, the answer came two months later, in the form of a retaliatory order designed to silence her once and for all. But the words spoken on January 28 remain, frozen in the transcript, waiting for anyone brave enough to read them.

title: Part 1: The Retaliatory Judge: How David Fried Uses the Bench to Punish Critics in Rockland County
content:
The notification arrived with the sterile, bureaucratic quiet typical of the New York State Courts Electronic Filing system. A ping in an inbox. A PDF attachment. To the untrained eye, the March 30, 2026, Decision and Order issued in the civil matter of Adler v. Pollak looked like the standard machinery of justice grinding through the docket. It carried the requisite caption, the index number—035769/2025—and the signature of David Fried, Acting Supreme Court Justice of Rockland County. But to read the document was to encounter something far more volatile than routine legal adjudication. It was a calculated act of professional destruction, draped in the robes of judicial authority.

For the plaintiff’s attorney, reading the order was an exercise in surrealism. She is a Muslim woman, a seasoned litigator who has spent her career navigating the intricate, often exclusionary corridors of the New York legal system. She knows the unspoken rules of the courtroom. She knows that a judge’s word is, for all practical purposes, law—at least until an appellate panel says otherwise. But what she found in Fried’s order was not law. It was a deeply personal, vitriolic attack, an attempt to permanently stain her professional reputation and chill any future dissent.

The order did not emerge in a vacuum. It was issued shortly after The Ethics Reporter filed a formal bar complaint detailing Fried’s abusive conduct during a January 28 inquest in the same case. It was a retaliatory strike, designed to inflict maximum damage while bypassing the procedural safeguards that are supposed to protect attorneys from capricious judges. Specifically, Fried explicitly declined to impose a formal sanction under Part 130 of the Rules of the Chief Administrator—a mechanism that requires an Order to Show Cause, adequate notice, and an opportunity to be heard. Instead, he twisted the procedural framework, holding the attorney and her law office jointly and severally liable for $3,500 in “costs” under CPLR § 6514(c).

The maneuver was as cynical as it was transparent. By labeling the financial penalty as “costs” under a specific statutory provision rather than a formal sanction, Fried attempted to circumvent the due process requirements of Part 130. More insidiously, he sought to insulate his conduct from appellate review, trapping the attorney in a procedural nightmare where the merits of his abusive behavior could not be easily challenged. It was a weaponization of the civil practice rules, a demonstration of what happens when a judge decides that his power is absolute and that any challenge to his authority must be met with overwhelming, punitive force.

To understand how a judge in a New York State courtroom feels emboldened to issue such a nakedly retaliatory order, one must look beyond the four corners of the document. One must look at the ecosystem that produced David Fried, the political machinery that elevated him, and the structural failures that allow judicial misconduct to flourish in plain sight.

Rockland County politics is an insular, transactional world. It is a place where favors are traded like currency, and where political survival often depends on an intricate web of alliances, willful blindness, and strategic amnesia. David Fried is a product of this environment. Long before Governor Kathy Hochul appointed him to the New York State Court of Claims in June 2023—a stepping stone to his current role as an Acting Supreme Court Justice—Fried was deeply embedded in the local political machine. He previously served as a justice in the Town of Ramapo and the Village of Spring Valley, jurisdictions notorious for their labyrinthine and often controversial political dynamics.

His ambition was never confined to the bench. In 2013, Fried ran for Rockland County Executive, a campaign that ultimately faltered but underscored his deep political aspirations. His political history, however, is not without its shadows. In 2009, he was embroiled in a scandal involving his campaign office space, which was linked to a notorious local slumlord—a controversy that raised early questions about his judgment and the company he kept. More recently, in February 2026, The Ethics Reporter broke the story of his connections to associates of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, revealing a network of relationships that seemed entirely incongruous with the ethical demands of judicial office.

Despite these red flags, the political establishment elevated him. He even spent time working as an Assistant to the Sheriff for Public Safety and Counterterrorism at the Rockland County Sheriff’s Office, padding his resume with law enforcement credentials that appealed to the region’s conservative-leaning voters. When Governor Hochul appointed him to the Court of Claims, the vetting process—which is supposed to rigorously examine a candidate’s temperament, background, and ethical fitness—somehow missed or ignored the warning signs. The appointment was a testament to the power of political patronage over rigorous scrutiny, a failure of the executive branch to safeguard the integrity of the judiciary.

The March 30 order is the inevitable result of this systemic failure. When a judge is elevated through political channels rather than rigorous meritocratic evaluation, and when he operates in a system where accountability is an illusion, the bench becomes a fiefdom. The judge ceases to be a neutral arbiter and becomes a petty tyrant. The courtroom transforms from a forum for justice into a stage for the exercise of raw power.

For attorneys practicing before such judges, the unwritten rule is absolute compliance. The structural silence imposed on the legal profession is profound. Lawyers are deeply reluctant to criticize judges, not out of respect for the institution, but out of fear for their clients and their livelihoods. A judge has the power to destroy a case with a single evidentiary ruling, to bankrupt a client with adverse decisions, and, as David Fried demonstrated, to defame an attorney in a public, permanent record.

This dynamic is exacerbated by the historical toothlessness of the New York State Commission on Judicial Conduct. Tasked with investigating and disciplining judges, the Commission has a reputation for moving slowly, acting cautiously, and reserving its most severe penalties for only the most egregious and undeniable instances of misconduct. For everyday bullying, for the subtle and not-so-subtle abuses of power that occur in courtrooms across the state, the Commission offers little solace and even less deterrence. Judges know this. They know that the odds of facing meaningful discipline for mistreating an attorney are vanishingly small.

When the Muslim attorney appeared before Fried on January 28—a hearing that would become the catalyst for the March 30 retaliation—she was stepping into this minefield. She had experienced a sudden, severe personal emergency the night before. Recognizing her obligation to the court and her client, she had submitted a letter explaining her situation and requesting an accommodation, ultimately appearing virtually via Microsoft Teams. What she expected was professional understanding, or at the very least, a dispassionate ruling on her request. What she received was an unhinged berating.

Fried attacked her not just for the letter, but for the audacity of experiencing a human crisis that inconvenienced his calendar. When, recognizing the hostility and the potential prejudice to her client, she attempted to fulfill her professional duty by preserving her objections on the record, Fried’s anger intensified. He attacked her for trying to “create a record,” treating the fundamental mechanism of appellate preservation as a personal insult. It was a display of judicial temperament so degraded that it prompted The Ethics Reporter to file the bar complaint.

The March 30 order, then, was Fried’s response to being challenged. It was a message not just to the attorney in Adler v. Pollak, but to the entire Rockland County bar. The message was clear: Dare to complain, dare to document my behavior, dare to expose my conduct to outside scrutiny, and I will use the full weight of my office to crush you.

By assessing $3,500 in “costs” under CPLR § 6514(c), Fried engaged in a sophisticated form of legal bullying. CPLR § 6514(c) is typically used to award costs and expenses when a Notice of Pendency (a lis pendens) is canceled. It is not a general-purpose tool for sanctioning attorneys for perceived slights during an inquest. But Fried weaponized it, distorting its purpose to inflict a financial penalty without triggering the due process protections of Part 130.

Under Part 130, a judge must issue an Order to Show Cause, giving the attorney notice of the specific conduct being scrutinized and an opportunity to present a defense. It requires an evidentiary basis and a formal finding of frivolous conduct. Fried bypassed all of this. He acted as accuser, prosecutor, judge, and jury, issuing his defamatory order unilaterally. He stripped the attorney of her right to defend herself, memorializing his vitriol in a public document that would haunt her career, and attaching a price tag to her dissent.

This is the anatomy of judicial retaliation. It is rarely as simple as a judge yelling in an empty room. It is usually dressed up in legal jargon, buried in procedural mechanisms, and stamped with the seal of the court. It is designed to look like the law, even as it subverts the very foundation of the legal system.

The attorney, reading the order in the quiet of her office, understood the gravity of what had just happened. This was no longer just about a civil dispute over a piece of property or a breach of contract. It was about the integrity of the profession. It was about whether a Muslim woman, fighting for her client, could be summary executed professionally by a judge with a history of scandal and a political machine guarding his flank.

The order sat there on the screen, a digital monument to judicial arrogance. It demanded a response, not just in the appellate courts, but in the court of public opinion. It demanded that the shadows of Rockland County politics be illuminated, that the failures of the vetting process be exposed, and that the structural silence of the bar be broken.

David Fried’s March 30 order was intended to be the final word, a crushing blow that would end the inquiry into his conduct. Instead, it was an admission of guilt, a desperate, retaliatory strike that revealed exactly who he is, and exactly why he is unfit to wear the robe.

title: Part 5: Judicial Misconduct in Plain Sight: Why David Fried Must Be Investigated by the Judicial Conduct Commission
content:
In the quiet, administrative corridors of New York state’s judicial oversight apparatus, grievances generally go to die in the dark. The New York State Commission on Judicial Conduct, headquartered in a nondescript office in Manhattan with branches upstate, receives thousands of complaints every year. They arrive in manila envelopes, in hastily typed emails, in frantic phone calls from litigants who believe they have been wronged and attorneys who know they have been abused. The vast majority of these complaints are dismissed outright. They are categorized as routine appellate issues, or the sour grapes of the losing party, or simply matters lacking the documentary weight required to challenge the state's most insulated class of public officials. For decades, the Commission has operated as a quiet pressure valve, maintaining the illusion of accountability while largely protecting the sanctity and the silence of the bench.

To understand the structural silence that surrounds the judiciary in New York, one must understand the unique vulnerability of the practicing attorney. A lawyer’s livelihood is entirely dependent on the grace of the court. To file a formal complaint against a sitting judge is to invite professional suicide. Judges talk. They attend the same bar association dinners, they golf at the same country clubs, they operate within a tightly knit fraternity that instinctively recoils at the sight of a whistleblower. An attorney who reports a judge for misconduct knows that they will likely have to appear before that same judge, or their colleagues, again. The retaliation is rarely overt. It is a motion denied here, a scheduling conflict ignored there, a subtle but pervasive chilling of the attorney’s practice. Because of this, the Commission on Judicial Conduct is starved of its most crucial resource: the testimony of the lawyers who actually witness the abuse.

This historical toothlessness has bred a specific kind of judicial hubris, nowhere more evident than in the political ecosystem of Rockland County, and nowhere more embodied than in the career of Acting Supreme Court Justice David Fried. Fried is a product of the Ramapo and Spring Valley political machine, an environment where political survival often requires a certain flexibility with the rules and a profound understanding of patronage. His trajectory is a testament to the fail-safes built into the New York political apparatus for those who play the game. In 2009, his campaign for local office operated out of a building owned by a notorious slumlord, a scandal that would have derailed a less connected politician. In 2013, he made a failed bid for Rockland County Executive. He later found a comfortable landing spot working as an Assistant to the Sheriff for Public Safety and Counterterrorism. Along the way, he cultivated connections with individuals deeply embedded in the county's power structures, including associates of the late Jeffrey Epstein—a revelation recently brought to light by The Ethics Reporter. Through it all, Fried remained untouched, eventually ascending to the Town of Ramapo and Village of Spring Valley justice courts.

Then came June 2023. In a move that highlighted the profound failures of the state's judicial vetting process, Governor Kathy Hochul appointed Fried to the New York State Court of Claims, effectively elevating him to Acting Supreme Court Justice. The appointment was a triumph of machine politics over rigorous scrutiny. It sent a clear message to the legal community of Rockland County: David Fried was untouchable. He had ascended to the highest trial court in the state, insulated by the Governor’s seal of approval and the historical inertia of the Commission on Judicial Conduct.

But hubris has a way of leaving a paper trail.

On the morning of January 28, 2026, the structural silence of the Rockland County courthouse fractured. The venue was a routine civil inquest in the matter of Adler v. Pollak, conducted via Microsoft Teams. The plaintiff’s attorney, a Muslim woman, had experienced a severe personal emergency the night before. Following standard protocol, she had submitted a letter to the court detailing the emergency and requesting an adjournment. In a functioning judicial system, such a request, especially from an attorney dealing with a sudden crisis, is handled with basic professional courtesy. But David Fried’s courtroom had long ceased to function on the principles of basic professional courtesy.

When the attorney appeared on the Teams screen, visibly distressed and attempting to manage her crisis while fulfilling her ethical obligation to her client, Judge Fried did not offer a continuance. Instead, he unleashed a barrage of hostility. He attacked her for submitting the letter. He berated her professional conduct. The transcript of the hearing reads less like a judicial proceeding and more like a targeted psychological assault. When the attorney, drawing on her training and attempting to protect her client’s rights for an inevitable appeal, calmly tried to preserve her objections on the record, Fried’s anger escalated. He attacked her explicitly for trying to "create a record." It was a naked display of judicial intimidation—an attempt to bully an attorney into abandoning her duty to her client out of fear of the judge’s wrath.

For most attorneys, the encounter would have ended there. They would have muted their microphones, swallowed their indignation, and advised their clients that the judge was simply having a bad day. They would have calculated the cost of filing a complaint with the Commission on Judicial Conduct, weighed it against their future in Rockland County, and chosen silence. It is the calculation that thousands of attorneys make every year. It is the calculation that allows judges like David Fried to operate with impunity.

But the attorney in Adler v. Pollak did not remain silent. And crucially, she was not alone. The Ethics Reporter obtained the transcripts and the correspondence, documenting the abuse not as hearsay, but as empirical fact. In February 2026, The Ethics Reporter published the details of the hearing, exposing the machinery of intimidation that Fried had utilized. More importantly, The Ethics Reporter bypassed the terrified local bar and filed a formal bar complaint against Judge Fried directly with the Commission on Judicial Conduct.

The filing of the complaint fundamentally altered the dynamic. The Commission was no longer dealing with the whispered complaints of an anonymous lawyer. They were presented with public, documentary evidence of a judge explicitly attacking an attorney for attempting to preserve an appellate record—a fundamental violation of a litigant's right to due process.

Faced with exposure, Judge Fried had a choice. He could have recused himself. He could have recognized that his temperament had compromised his impartiality. Instead, he chose retaliation. On March 30, 2026, David Fried issued a Decision and Order that will likely serve as the defining document of his judicial downfall.

The March 30 order was not a legal document; it was a weaponized tantrum disguised in the formatting of a judicial decree. In it, Fried explicitly defamed the plaintiff's attorney, twisting the events of January 28 into a narrative that portrayed him as the victim of her supposed insolence. But the true malice of the order lay in its mechanism of punishment. Fried wanted to sanction the attorney, to inflict financial pain for her audacity. However, formal sanctions under Part 130 of the New York Codes, Rules and Regulations require due process: an Order to Show Cause, formal notice, and a hearing where the attorney can defend herself.

Fried knew he could not survive a public hearing. He knew that if he issued an Order to Show Cause, the attorney would have the opportunity to present the transcript of the January 28 hearing, exposing his abuse on the record. So, he bypassed the rules entirely. He declined to issue a Part 130 sanction. Instead, he contorted a completely unrelated statute—Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) Section 6514(c), a provision designed for the cancellation of a Notice of Pendency in real estate disputes—to hold the attorney and her law office jointly and severally liable for $3,500 in opposing counsel's "costs."

By issuing the March 30 order, Judge Fried committed the cardinal sin of corrupt authority: he put the corruption in writing. The order was explicitly retaliatory, legally nonsensical, and procedurally void. It was an attempt to use the financial power of the bench to punish an attorney who had dared to report him, and to do so in a way that circumvented her right to appeal the conduct through normal disciplinary channels.

This is why the Commission on Judicial Conduct finds itself at a historical crossroads. For decades, it has justified its inaction by pointing to the ambiguity of the complaints it receives. It has hidden behind the veil of "judicial discretion," arguing that a judge's temperament on the bench is a matter of personal style rather than ethical misconduct. But there is no ambiguity in the transcript of January 28. There is no judicial discretion in the retaliatory order of March 30.

David Fried has handed the Commission the exact type of documentary evidence it has always claimed it requires to take action. He has demonstrated, on the public record, a fundamental inability to separate his personal vindictiveness from his judicial duties. He has shown that he views the law not as a framework for justice, but as a blunt instrument to be wielded against those who challenge his authority. If the Commission fails to act on this—if it looks at the transcripts, the retaliatory order, the explicit weaponization of the CPLR, and decides that David Fried is still fit to wear the robe—then it is not merely toothless. It is complicit.

The machinery of silence in New York's judiciary relies on the assumption that nobody is watching, and that even if they are, nobody cares. David Fried operated under that assumption for his entire political and judicial career. He survived the slumlord scandals, the Epstein connections, the machine politics of Ramapo. He was elevated by the Governor. He believed he was immune. But the ink on the March 30 order is dry, and the transcripts cannot be unwritten. The shadows where grievances usually go to die have been flooded with light, leaving the judge, and the Commission that oversees him, with nowhere left to hide.

title: Part 3: From Slumlords to Epstein Associates: The Troubling Political History of Rockland County Judge David Fried
content:
In the geometry of local politics, the campaign headquarters is a temporary citadel. It is usually a leased storefront with papered-over windows, smelling faintly of stale coffee, industrial carpet adhesive, and the desperate, electric hum of ambition. It is meant to be the physical embodiment of a candidate’s promise to the community—a beacon of accessibility and forward momentum. But in 2013, when David Fried was mounting his campaign for Rockland County Executive, his political architecture was built on a foundation that was, quite literally, decaying. The space he occupied was owned by a figure notorious in the local community as a slumlord, a landlord whose properties were synonymous with peeling paint, chronic violations, and the silent suffering of tenants who had nowhere else to go. This was not merely a matter of poor real estate judgment; it was a profound illustration of the cognitive dissonance required to climb the ladder of New York municipal politics.

To understand David Fried’s eventual ascent to the bench of the New York State Supreme Court, one must first understand the geography of his origins. Rockland County is a complex ecosystem, a dense, suburban wedge caught between the gravitational pull of New York City and the sprawling quiet of the Hudson Valley. Within it lie the Town of Ramapo and the Village of Spring Valley, jurisdictions that have long operated as insular fiefdoms. Here, politics is less about grand ideological battles than it is about the raw, unglamorous mechanics of patronage, demographic alliances, and the strategic distribution of favors. In this environment, a candidate does not rise purely on the merits of their jurisprudence or the purity of their civic vision. They rise because they understand how the machine works, and more importantly, they understand whom the machine protects.

The 2013 slumlord scandal was a flare illuminating the dark machinery of Fried’s political operation. When you are running to be the chief executive of a county, every association is a signal. Renting space from a landlord known for exploiting the county's most vulnerable residents sent a clear, if unintentional, message about where the campaign’s true allegiances lay. The optics were disastrous, but in the cloistered world of Ramapo politics, optics are often treated as an inconvenience rather than a disqualification. The controversy, which had roots stretching back to his earlier political maneuvers in 2009, revealed a candidate who was deeply embedded in a network of power brokers who operated with impunity. It was an early warning sign of a foundational arrogance—a belief that the rules of accountability, the very expectations of ethical hygiene, simply did not apply to him.

This network of alliances extended into shadows that were only fully brought to light years later. In February 2026, The Ethics Reporter broke the story of Fried’s connections to associates of Jeffrey Epstein. The revelation was jarring, yet to observers of the Rockland County machine, it possessed a grim, inevitable logic. The Epstein network was, at its core, an enterprise that preyed upon vulnerability while insulating itself through proximity to power. How does a local political figure brush against such an apparatus? It happens not in grand conspiracies, but in the mundane transactions of campaign finance, the quiet dinners, the introductions made in the VIP tents of charity galas. It happens when a political ecosystem becomes so insular, so detached from the scrutiny of a robust press, that the only currency that matters is influence, regardless of its origin. The fact that these connections remained submerged for so long speaks volumes about the structural silence that protects the ambitious in local politics.

Following the bruising realities of electoral campaigns, Fried needed to launder his public image. He needed a title that conferred unimpeachable authority, something that washed away the grit of slumlord landlords and backroom deals. He found it in the Rockland County Sheriff’s Office. The title he acquired was almost comically grandiose: Assistant to the Sheriff for Public Safety and Counterterrorism. In a county primarily known for its shopping malls and commuter rail lines, "Counterterrorism" sounds less like a functional job description and more like a line of dialogue from a network television procedural. Yet, it served a vital, transformative purpose. It allowed Fried to wrap himself in the solemn, unquestionable mantle of law enforcement. The badge, the proximity to the machinery of state security, the gravitas of "public safety"—all of it acted as an ablution. He was no longer just a political operative navigating the messy terrain of Ramapo; he was a guardian of the public order.

This transition was the necessary prelude to his ultimate destination: the judiciary. Fried’s judicial career began in the subterranean levels of the justice system—as a justice for the Town of Ramapo and the Village of Spring Valley. Local justice courts are the basements of American jurisprudence. They are often crowded, poorly lit rooms where the dockets are punishingly long and the stakes, while seemingly small, are life-altering for the people involved. Evictions, traffic violations, small claims, minor infractions—these are the venues where the state exercises its power over the working class, the poor, and the marginalized. In these rooms, a judge wields a kind of absolute, unquestioned authority that rarely exists in higher courts. There are no appellate lawyers hovering in the gallery; there is rarely even a court reporter to document the nuance of a ruling or the tone of a reprimand.

It is in these local courts that judicial temperament is either forged or corrupted. For a judge inclined toward arrogance, the local bench is a dangerous training ground. It teaches them that their word is absolute law, that their impatience is justified, and that the people standing before them are not citizens seeking justice, but annoyances clogging the schedule.
